sentences.text: So--
--he asked me about my family.
Then he asked me, did I go to school?
And I told him that I finished a junior college.
He said, oh, you're an intelligent boy.
Would you like to study?
I said, yes, I would like to study.
He said he has contact in Kiev, Ukraine.
He has contact with a medical school.
Would you like to?
So I said I was supposed to study medicine in-- in Paris.
He said, you know what?
I have contact.
I will give you a letter, and you don't need a ticket.
I will write it down.
And you go with a train to Kiev.
I took off, and I went to Kiev.
I went to Kiev, and I registered myself to the medical school.
They put me also part time in a factory, that I should have a-- where to sleep.
And food I got there.
So I did almost nothing.
I worked with-- been filing in the office, you see, and around the factory a little bit.
But I didn't like it there.
The people, I mean, were drab-- everybody gray, angry-- not too much laughing.
They were afraid to talk to each other.
It's-- and I felt a-- some kind-- under the-- some kind anti-Semitic feeling there.
There was-- you know.
Officially, by law, you couldn't call a Jew, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was [NON-ENGLISH].
That's official.
Because if someone would call a derogatory name, a Jew, he could go to jail at that time, before 1939-- oh, at '39.
So-- but I felt, also, you see-- because sometimes they didn't know that I'm Jewish over there.
And they used to sing and express themselves quite a bit.
And I could see that there's, you know, some kind of a anti-Semitic kind of feeling.
And I was home sick.
Besides that, every Sunday I used to go on the main railroad station in Kiev, because there was a lot of transport going of people from the Ukraine of those refugees to work.
And I met one.
I used to go around.
I said, Kielce, Kielce, Kielce.
And there was a Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH], a gynecologist, who used to work with my mother.
And he said that before he left-- because the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
So I said, the-- the border was not yet solid.
They told him that I went some-- east.
If he sees me, he should tell me I should go home-- that they are crying-- I should go home.
He told me that.
And this was the impetus to go.
So I went to the office, and I said, do you know what?
I got the news that my family is in the Western Ukraine-- not in the German area, but in the Russian occupied area.
So he said they didn't want-- in the beginning, they didn't want to give me my documents.
He said, you study a 1/2 year.
Where are you going?
I said, I will come.
Maybe I go to Lwow.
You know, there was only-- was in Lwow-- later on.
So they gave me the documents.
Then I went to Lwow.
And then I went to Lwow.
There was also a place there where the refugees used to congregate.
There was a [NON-ENGLISH] a-- a coffee house-- [NON-ENGLISH].
That's a kind of a mushroom name.
And over there, the supervisor of this [NON-ENGLISH], and why the people from Kielce did congregate there, was a-- a soldier.
He was a warrant officer who was Jewish.
Nobody knew about it.
In Kielce, it was Jewish.
And he was also a supervisor in a movie, which belonged to the fourth regiment of infantry, which was stationed in our city.
So he was the supervisor, and we knew it.
So the Kielce people used to congregate there.
And I went to this [NON-ENGLISH] there.
And I used to make-- I used to make a few rubles by standing in the line and buying vodka-- because you couldn't buy any place else-- and then resell it, you see.
And over there, I met some other people who were thinking about going back to German-occupied Poland.
And over there I met Mr.
Rubinstein, who's alive.
He's here in Chicago.
He met me there.
And we decided to go back to the German-occupied area.
We had to smuggle ourselves.
It was winter.
It was very cold.
The snow was very high.
But the [NON-ENGLISH] River, which was the border between the German and Russian-occupied area, was frozen.
And at night, we went, and we smuggled ourselves through.
And what happened-- we knew that everybody-- Jewish population-- everybody from about 10 years old-- had to wear a armband-- a white armband with a blue [NON-ENGLISH], OK-- a star of David.
We made ourselves those armbands in Lwow.
And when we came over the border, we were on a sled, and we put those on.
And on the way, we didn't realize that Gestapo, or whatever-- I don't know.
The first time I saw those people with the bones, you know, and the, you know-- skull-- started to beat us up-- very bad with truncheons-- sitting on-- you know, we were sitting on the-- on the sled.
And they took us to the railroad station to unload the briquettes-- coal briquettes-- to put them in the truck-- on the wagons, you see?
And they let us go after that.
And then we were frozen.
There was not far a bakery.
So we went to the bakery to warm up a little bit.
And the people looked at us, and they said, you crazy people.
What did you do?
What did you come here for?
It's a tragedy to be here.
But again, I-- I couldn't go anymore back.
I started to trickle back to Kielce.
Did you have your papers with you?
Yes.
I have my papers with me.
And I came to Kielce, and I came home.

But I have to be clandestine, because if the Germans would find out that I was on the other side and came here, oh, they would kill me, or they would send me to Auschwitz, or to the concentration camp.
So for a period of time, I didn't do anything-- except Dr.
Peltz, who used to be the-- I don't know-- president.
He was the [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, the-- He was the head of the Judenrat.
--head of the Judenrat, OK?
Outstanding man, OK?
He knew me, and he knew my family very well-- my mother.
He, after a little while, when it quieted down and everything, you know-- started to show my face.
He gave me a job in the [NON-ENGLISH] Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] OK, in the office-- you know, some filing, and something like that, you see?
And I was there.
I was there until they took him-- and then to Auschwitz-- Dr.
Peltz.
He didn't go-- he didn't cooperate.
He committed suicide in Auschwitz.
He committed suicide.
Yeah.
Not yet.
Not yet.
What happened-- my-- my grandmother used to be there.
And also, we had to accept now in our apartment a couple who was thrown out from Germany, from Vienna, from Austria.
So we have to-- they were assigned to our apartment, OK?
Three-- three rooms and a kitchen, OK?
And-- but what happened, you see, the Germans, at that time, made [NON-ENGLISH]-- a quarantine.
Typhus started to-- to outbreak, you see?
And so, what they did, they put some barbed wire around a special area of the city.
Nobody could move in or out.
You see, that's all.
That was before the ghetto.
It was right in 1940, OK?
And I worked in this place, then, [NON-ENGLISH], until Dr.
Peltz went.
I lost the pool, and Mr.
Levy got to be the president of the Judenrat.
And I lost the job.
Except they let me work in the office-- not the office-- but I used to direct the people where to go, what department, what office, and so forth.
Because there was quite a traffic.
That's what they-- they got.
And when I worked there, my mother could work.
She worked as a-- as a midwife, yet, OK?
In the hospital?
In the hospital and out of the hospital-- the homes too.
My father used to also supervise a group of about 350 Jewish people who where going to Zagnansk.
This is a town-- not a town-- a village-- about maybe 1/2 an hour by railroad-- going to a quarry.
Those Jewish people worked in a quarry, and he was the supervisor.
He was the-- the man who was in charge.
Zagnansk, Z-A-G-N-A-N-S-K, near Kielce-- and about 350 people.
In fact, he used to get a supply of marmalade, and so forth.
And they used to come to our apartment to distribute this stuff, you see, for the-- for the people who used to work.
So then I worked, and I didn't like this kind of a job.
So what they announced-- my mother-- my sister works in the hospital too.
She was not a  nurse, but she worked around-- some clean clothes or something like that.
I have a picture of her when she works there.
And my mother told me, she said, there's a course in-- in medical course, here in the hospital.
You wanted always to be a doctor.
Maybe you take this course here and be some-- work something in medicine.
So I said fine.
So I took the course.
It was a very hard course.
And I passed this course.
And I got to be a-- they call [NON-ENGLISH].
It's some kind of-- with sanitation.
My job was to go from home to home and destroy the vermin, or lice, you see, and louse, you see, too-- too-- and not to-- because the lice were the carrier of the typhus.
And I didn't like it either.
And I decided with this friend of mine, Mosberg, we should go work in the factory.
Because they started already to talk at that time-- it was already later.
I mean, I am advancing a little bit the time.
Because there was a period of time I was the [NON-ENGLISH].
And we decided to go to work to-- in a factory.
And there were two factories-- sister factories-- by us-- Ludvikov and Henrykov.
Ludvikov-- L-U-D-V-I-K-O-V, because phonetically-- and Henrykov was H-E-N-R-Y-K-O-V.
They were sister plant.
One was metal-- metal producing-- you know-- factory; the other one wood-- wood.

And mainly, during the-- by 1941, they made-- they produced the horse-drawn wagon for the Russian campaign, because the tanks couldn't do-- you know-- they couldn't move through the bogs in-- in Ukraine and so forth, you see?
So we decided to work, me and Mosberg.
The Jake Mosberg, who survived, is now in Florida.
And we started to work.
And I started work in Ludvikov.
And we used to go every day with a column, you know, five front breast, you see, every day under guard, to the-- to the factory and come back home.
And there I worked.
And meanwhile--
Yes, yeah.
It was a volunteer.
Otherwise, we had to work some other-- everybody had to work something.
But you had to sign up for it.
Yes, yeah, ah-ha, under guard.
And because you-- I'm talking about ghetto already, you see?
Because you understand what I'm talking about?
I'm advancing myself a little bit.
Okay, fine.
First of all, there was [NON-ENGLISH].
It didn't last too long.
I think six months or so was the quarantine, OK?
I think by Passover, 1941, they took the Jewish population, crowded them in a small area on the northwest side of the city, in a very, very poor neighborhood.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our-- what happened-- our building was right kitty corner from the City Hall.
Our building was excluded from the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we had to leave.
We had to move out from-- from our apartment.
We had to leave everything what we had there.
And they put on-- us on the Jasna-- on a street by the name of Jasna-- J-A-S-S-N-A-- J-A-S--- J-A-S-N-A.
There was an apartment-- of a kitchen and one room.
And me, my father, my mother, and my sister had one room.
And they put another people in the kitchen.
We lost those two German people-- Jewish couple-- because they had to accommodate them someplace else.
And over there, we stayed until August 1942, until the Treblinka time.
Yes, [NON-ENGLISH]-- ghetto [NON-ENGLISH].
And in ghetto, I was also going to work to Ludvikov.
Oh, there was a-- a president of the-- of the Judenrat, Dr.
Levy.
Oh the Germans was-- they was like, by us, there were Schupo-- Schutzpolizei.
You know?
There was Gestapo.
And those are mainly-- mainly the people.
Now the Schutzpolizei was Hauptmann Geyer--
G-E-Y-E-R.
He was a captain.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
OK?
I think the Shupo came from Graz in Austria, because all the murderers came from there, you see.
Because Rumpel, was the biggest murderer in our town-- was from that city.
I think that he was a Shupo.
The Gestapo was Thomas-- T-H-O-M-A-S, Thomas.
Hanisch-- there were a lot-- different names, you know.
But there was the chief, Thomas.
Then when Thomas left, there was a fellow by the name of Essig, E-S-S-I-G, because I have to recall him later on when I was already in the camp.
And those people did whatever they wanted with us.
They used to come to the ghetto and kill people, beat them up with dogs, you know, break windows, break the houses, arrest.
They forced the Jewish policemen, or they even-- they would have a Polish policemen do a lot of damage to the people.
And--
--except for-- for a little bit lack of food, in the ghetto, no-- not my close family, no.
I worked.
I used to get lunch at the factory.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No-- no wages at all.
We didn't.
The food-- I-- I don't recall exactly was I hungry or not-- in the ghetto, myself.
I-- I don't really recall that I was starved.
I was hungry, but I didn't starve.
And I worked under very primitive conditions.
I worked, for example, with blasting sand.
I didn't have any mask.
I used to take a handkerchief over my nose, put it here, and that was-- the thing.
I worked on a lathe, a metal lathe-- nothing, you know?
There-- there was no protection at all.
We worked very hard.
There was no set hours.
We could work day-- 12 hours, 8 hours, 10 hours.
On special Jewish holidays we did double kind of work.
That was in Ludvikov.
Then I was transferred later on to the sister plant, to Henrykov, to the wooden production.
But that was already after they liquidated the ghetto.
Yes, yeah.
For example, like on Purim, I think it was-- it was '43 or 4-- let's see-- or '42?
They used to take the doctors-- intelligence.
They used to take people who they thought they were communists before the war-- the Germans.

They used to take people who make some minor breaking of the law-- let's say, like smuggling, trying to buy something from the Poles through the ghetto, let's say, through the-- through the fences, or through the barbed wire.
They used to beat up people.
They used to take the people at night to the jail and send them away to-- without-- nobody knew where.
They used to-- there was a jail on [NON-ENGLISH].
There was a Polish jail in the city.
And they used to put them to this jail, temporarily.
And then from there, they used to take them to Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They don't come back.
No, they didn't come back.
No, no.
They didn't make-- physically they didn't make it smaller.
But there were about 28,000 people there, and maybe the-- the population could be accepted, maybe 1/10 of it.
It were very, very crowded, you see?
For example, like from our-- we had six people-- two people there in the kitchen, and our four people in one room, in our simple two room.
I mean, a room and a kitchen-- we used to live.
But this, we stayed there until August the 20th, 1942.
Right.
Oh, I-- I listen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before Treblinka, I knew there's-- we have-- we had a desperate situation.
The situation was desperate.
I never expected the Germans to be that strong.
I could see, because the railroad was going not far from our place in ghetto, I saw what they-- what they prepared themselves for Russia.
I see all the tanks and all the guns that they carried, you see, towards Warsaw.
Because there was Krakow, Warsaw, you see, the railroads, you know.
And when they attacked Russia, they started to go east so rapidly and take-- took so many armies-- destroyed so many armies, destroyed so many, and took prisoners, us prisoners.
I thought that they will never be destroyed.
I thought they would never let-- that nobody will be able to help us.
And when I started to make contact, already at that time, my uncle, who used to be an officer in-- in the Polish army before the war, he had some contact.
And-- and his brother-in-law, who was a member with him of [NON-ENGLISH], the-- you know-- they were revisionists, you know, from [NON-ENGLISH].
They organized in my area a group of Jewish people who were revisionists.
And they thought about fighting or going to Romania, smuggle themselves, you see, that way, and so forth.
They tried to do something, but the hostile population-- the hostile population in Poland prevented many, many people who maybe could accomplish a lot, from surviving, or doing even that.
August 1942, yes.
August 1492-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --at about 3 o'clock or 4 o'clock, they started to knock-- the Jewish police started to knock on our door.
We knew something will happen.
August 20th-- August 20.
We knew that something will happen, because there used to be an outfit by the name of Sliwa, S-L-I-W-A-- where they use a woodworking outfit.
And they used to make small ramps for the railroad-- steps-- to get in, you know.
And there was some rumors that they will take the people and send them east.
They will evacuate us to work someplace there, by train.
So we thought that they will go there and to-- we work some-- east.
So we were waiting.
So when the time-- they surrounded the city.
The Polish police, Ukrainians, SS, Gestapo, they surrounded the area.
And there was a special place-- oh, they knocked on the door.
And even I understood Russian, but the Ukrainians talk Ukrainians.
[NON-ENGLISH], you know-- get up, and get out, you see?
And they started to beat the people.
People started to take with them a lot of stuff.
And they had to lose it, because they-- you know, you couldn't walk.
They-- they beat you, and they throw you.

And my father had to prepare before, a special place there on [NON-ENGLISH] Street, which was like a quadrangle-- a big quadrangle, where they did accumulate all those concentrated-- all those people.
They split the city in three areas for the evacuation.
Ours was the first-- the first one.
And so they prepared this area.
There was grass and something growing to cut it, you know?
He was in charge of it-- to cut it and make it ready.
So we knew that will happen-- something will happen.
Everybody had a bundle or something, to take with himself-- with him.
And when we went to the place there, it was real chaos.
There was people laying already killed, and destroyed, and crying, and children lost, and then packages, and valises, and tanks around.
It was really something tragic.
I didn't see my father anymore.
I was with my mother and my sister.
And we worked, you know, around.
They worked in the-- in the hospital.
We worked together.
And I worked in the-- in the-- in the factory.
So we had-- everybody had a special, [NON-ENGLISH] you see? that was a-- a document-- internal document, you know?
And they started to make a-- they took us to the place by the central synagogue, OK?
And there was the selection.
Geyer and Thomas were there.
And you had to walk.
And they ask you to show the document-- where do you work, you see?
So my mother said, I am in the hospital.
My sister and I-- OK, you go there.
Where do you work?
I said, Ludvikov.
Oh, go with them, right there, OK.
If they-- older people, children, women, right away to the other side, OK?
So I was left with my mother and my sister.
My father was not there anymore.
So they went in the temple, in the synagogue, to rest.
And I was still around, you see.
And suddenly, they surrounded me.
Some Ukrainians surrounded me by the temple there.
And they said, you go to work.
So I said, what kind of work?
You go to work.
I spoke Russian.
You say, you will work there.
They took us.
The SS came to them, and they said like that.
You're going to work there.
You will put some dead people in the grave to prevent a-- a-- disease, you know.
We should not let the disease spread.
You won't get killed.
You will quit your work.
And you go back.
So we took us under guard there.
And they made artificial grave.
No, they made a-- two graves.
They took a big-- four, you know, like a square-- big graves.
When I came, already, I had relieved-- our group relieved already, a group which worked there.
And I saw that they took them back to the synagogue.
And over there was the deeper graves.
And there were two SS men sitting on chairs, like a kitchen chairs.
And in front of them was a white linen, you know, like a bed linen, you see?
And on the linen was money, gold, rings, valuables, you know, and-- and pieces selected there, you see?
And wagons-- horse drawn wagons-- used to bring the people, dead people, from surrounding area.
And we could hear guns, you know, salvos and single guns all over around there, you see?
They used to bring it.
Our function was like that-- to take off the people from the wagons.
Take off the clothes.
Check everything if there's anything valuable, OK?
Take off any rings, gold teeth, you know, to tear out, and throw them in.
And put them in layers.
My function, personally, was-- I was supposed to open a quicklime.
They came-- the quicklime came in-- in-- bags of heavy paper, brown paper, you see?
And it was closed with a wire on the end.
So what happened-- I kneeled down and started to open this-- this-- wire.
And the SS man kicked me.
And he said, you son.
You know, he said, you goddamn Jew.
I show you how to open this.
He took the-- a shovel and cut this thing through-- 1/2 and 1/2.
And the-- the lime came out.
And he started to-- to show me.
And when he kicked me, I fell in in the-- in the grave there.
And he started to throw the stuff on me.
And I started to sneeze, and cough, and choke because of the dust, you know, from the quicklime.
And I thought they will kill me right there.
But they didn't kill me.
And a signal of this chief there of this group-- he sent another fellow to give me the hand.
And they pulled me out from the grave.
